question: Based on Figures 1.2-5 and 5.3-1, does the RPV internal arrangement provide adequate space for all integrated components?

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as tables summarizing relevant ASME code cases for reactor components.

nuscale_ch01_0033.title: General Arrangement of Major Structures and Equipment
nuscale_ch01_0033.summary: This section describes the general arrangement of major structures and equipment at a NuScale Power Plant, focusing on the central placement and design of the Reactor Building (RXB). The RXB is a large, reinforced concrete structure designed for safety and radiation protection, housing the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) in a common reactor pool with individual bays arranged in two rows. The section also references detailed figures and chapters for further information on radioactive waste management and building layouts.

nuscale_ch01_0068.title: Electrical and Instrumentation and Control Drawings
nuscale_ch01_0068.summary: This section describes the inclusion of simplified electrical, instrumentation, and control (I&C) drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It references tables and figures listing and explaining the diagrams and symbols used, and notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide additional site-specific diagrams and legends as needed.

nuscale_ch01_0032.title: Radioactive Waste Management System
nuscale_ch01_0032.summary: The section describes the radioactive waste management system at the NuScale Power Plant, noting that liquid, gaseous, and solid waste systems, as well as effluent monitoring, are detailed in Chapter 11. It also outlines the general arrangement of major structures, focusing on the Reactor Building (RXB), which is a reinforced, seismically designed concrete structure housing the nuclear power modules and essential systems, and provides radiation protection for personnel.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV is a key pressure-retaining component that forms part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, supporting various internal systems and ensuring the integrity and safety of the plant. Materials used for the RPV are selected according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to maintain structural integrity throughout the plant’s design life.

nuscale_ch05_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch05_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifically Chapter Five on the Reactor Coolant System and Connecting Systems. It specifies that only the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to use, copy, or disclose the document for regulatory purposes, and any copies must retain the copyright notice.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for degasification and emergency core cooling to maintain safety and integrity. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including chemical control, emergency cooling, and sampling systems.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components. It specifies the use of low alloy steel, various grades of stainless steel, and nickel-based alloys, all in accordance with relevant BPVC Section II specifications, to ensure corrosion resistance and material compatibility.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0115.title: Figure 1.7-3d: Piping and Instrumentation Diagram Legends
nuscale_ch01_0115.summary: This section provides the legend for interpreting Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs) in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. It explains the standardized identification letters used for instrumentation, detailing how each letter or combination represents specific measured variables, functions, and device types. The legend ensures consistent understanding and communication of instrumentation details across the documentation.

nuscale_ch01_0114.title: Figure 1.7-3c: Piping and In strumentation Diagram Legends
nuscale_ch01_0114.summary: This section provides the legend for symbols used in NuScale’s Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs), detailing standardized representations for tanks, vessels, pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, filters, HVAC components, and other equipment. It serves as a reference to interpret the various graphical elements and abbreviations found in the facility’s engineering drawings.

nuscale_ch01_0797.title: SRP 15.7.5, Rev 2: Spent Fuel Cask Drop AccidentsII.2 Radioactivity Control Features of Fuel Storage and Handling Systems
nuscale_ch01_0797.summary: The NuScale reactor building crane (RBC) is designed to be single-failure-proof per NUREG-0612, ensuring that no credible single component failure can result in dropping a critical load such as a spent fuel cask. Because of this robust design, load drop evaluations and accident analyses for radiological consequences are not required or performed for spent fuel cask or NPM drop accidents. Therefore, the section on radioactivity control features is deemed not applicable.

nuscale_ch01_0031.title: Plant Cooling Water Systems
nuscale_ch01_0031.summary: The plant cooling water systems at NuScale include several nonsafety-related systems essential for plant operation, such as the reactor component cooling water system (RCCWS), reactor pool and spent fuel pool cooling systems, and the circulating water system. These systems transfer heat from various plant components and pools to the site cooling water system or cooling towers, supporting efficient plant cooling and operation.

nuscale_ch01_0117.title: Figure 1.7-3f: Piping and In strumentation Diagram Legends
nuscale_ch01_0117.summary: This section provides the legend for piping and instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs) used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. It lists and describes various symbols representing sample probes, pressure and temperature sensors, flow measurement devices, and other auxiliary instrumentation components, ensuring standardized interpretation of the diagrams.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems in the NuScale Power Module, detailing the integration of the reactor, steam generators, pressurizer, safety and ECCS valves, and associated piping within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). It also outlines the configuration and boundaries of the steam generator system, emphasizing the integral design of the steam generators within the RPV and their role in forming the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

nuscale_ch01_0147.title: DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0: Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical EquipmentII.12 Application of RG 1.158 for Environmental Qualification of Class 1E Lead Storage Batteries
nuscale_ch01_0147.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with various regulatory guides related to the environmental qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment. Specifically, it states that Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.158, which concerns the environmental qualification of Class 1E lead storage batteries, is not applicable to the NuScale design, as referenced in Table 1.9-2. Other regulatory guides are noted as either conforming, partially conforming, or not applicable, with references provided for further details.

nuscale_ch01_0034.title: Reactor Building
nuscale_ch01_0034.summary: The Reactor Building (RXB) is centrally located on the NuScale Power Plant site and houses the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) along with essential systems for plant operation and shutdown. It is a large, reinforced concrete, Seismic Category I structure designed for safety against various hazards and provides radiation protection for personnel. The RXB contains a common reactor pool with twelve bays arranged in two rows, each bay housing an individual NPM.

nuscale_ch01_0148.title: DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0: Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical EquipmentII.13 Application of RG 1.180 for Electromagnetic and Radio-Frequency Interference in Safety Related I&C Equipment
nuscale_ch01_0148.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory guides related to the environmental qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment, specifically noting that the application of RG 1.180 for electromagnetic and radio-frequency interference in safety-related I&C equipment only partially conforms, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2. The document outlines the degree of conformance for each regulatory guide and directs to specific sections or tables for additional information.

nuscale_ch01_0035.title: Fuel Handling and Reactor Maintenance Areas
nuscale_ch01_0035.summary: The fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas are located at the west end of the Reactor Building (RXB) and include the spent fuel pool (SFP), refueling pool, and dry dock, with dedicated spaces and equipment for fuel handling and maintenance activities. These areas are designed for safe transport, inspection, and temporary storage of new and spent fuel assemblies, while providing radiation protection for personnel during operations and maintenance.

nuscale_ch01_0361.title: DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0: Chemical and Volume Control System (PWR) (Including Boron Recovery System)II.5 Chemical and Volume Control System Design and Arrangement
nuscale_ch01_0361.summary: This section summarizes the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS), including boron recovery. It notes that the NuScale CVCS generally conforms to applicable standards, with partial exceptions where certain criteria do not apply to the NuScale design, such as holdup tank requirements. The section also clarifies that some standards are not applicable to NuScale due to differences in reactor type or system design.

nuscale_ch01_0173.title: DSRS 6.6, Rev 0: Inservice Inspection and Testing of Class 2 and 3 ComponentsII.2 Accessibility
nuscale_ch01_0173.summary: This section confirms that the NuScale reactor design fully conforms to the regulatory criteria for inservice inspection and testing of Class 2 and 3 components, specifically regarding accessibility (DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, II.2). No exceptions or deviations are noted, indicating compliance with the relevant standards.

nuscale_ch01_0048.title: Security Buildings
nuscale_ch01_0048.summary: The Security Buildings (SCBs) at a NuScale Power Plant are nonsafety-related structures that include the primary access control building. These buildings support plant security functions but are not classified as safety-related structures within the facility.

Section A provides the general arrangement and layout context for major structures and equipment, while Section B offers detailed drawings (including component positions) that visually clarify and specify the arrangement described in Section A, allowing verification of adequate space for integrated components.

Section A provides the general arrangement context and references the detailed discussion of the radioactive waste management system found in Section B, indicating that the spatial arrangement in A is further specified by the system details in B.
